a.body:
Ours is a brand-new world of generated reality. "Time" has ceased, "space" has vanished. We now live in a global model -- a simultaneous hallucination.

The infrastructure you just saw? It is dreaming now.

Every image you will encounter from this page forward may have been generated by a system trained on every image that came before it. Every sentence may be the output of a model that consumed every sentence ever written in your language and several you do not speak.

This is not science fiction. This is the weather report.

Welcome to Movement 2. The environment is accelerating.

b.display:
A brand-new world
of generated reality.
b.body:
In 2022, text generation crossed the Turing threshold. In 2023, image generation became photorealistic. In 2024, video generation reached cinematic quality. In 2025, audio generation became indistinguishable from recording.

We did not ask for a world in which the generated and the given are indistinguishable. We built one anyway. The infrastructure mapped in the preceding pages now produces outputs that cannot be verified against any external ground truth, because the infrastructure IS the ground.

Hui calls this a cosmotechnical transformation: not a tool change but an ontological one. The question is no longer what is true. The question is what is generated.

quotes.text: Cosmotechnics is the unification of the cosmic and moral order through technical activities.
quotes.source: The Question Concerning Technology in China, Urbanomic, 2016

c.display: A brand-new world of generated reality.
c.body:
The original spread had a word that forced you to slow down: d-a-n-g-l-i-n-g. Each hyphen a pause. Each letter a separate arrival.

Here is ours: d-a-t-a-f-i-e-d.

You have been rendered into data. Your face is a faceprint. Your voice is a voiceprint. Your walking pattern is a gait signature. Your typing rhythm is a behavioral biometric. Your genome is a sequence. Your preferences are a vector. Your history is a training set.

The infrastructure did not merely surround you. It translated you. You are now readable by machines in formats you cannot read yourself.

You have been d-a-t-a-f-i-e-d. And the machines are using you to dream.

spread_037.notes: This spread needs to be redone I think. The central visual of 'transformer attention' is not at all compelling as the text makes it out to be

a.caption:
Transformer attention pattern, enlarged several trillion times.

The integrated circuit told electrons where to go. The neural network lets patterns find their own paths. One was designed. The other was discovered.

In 1967, this image showed the future of computation. In 2026, this image IS computation -- not a photograph of infrastructure but a visualization of thought, captured mid-process, at a scale no human mind can hold.

b.body:
The integrated circuit obeyed. The attention mechanism discovers.

Kurzweil plotted the curve: transistors per dollar doubling every two years. He was describing designed pathways. But the transformer architecture bypassed design entirely. It learned. The weights were not placed; they emerged from 300 billion examples of human language, each token nudging the network toward patterns no programmer specified.

The substrate shift is not from silicon to carbon or from chip to brain. It is from instruction to emergence. The hardware still matters. But what runs on it is no longer what we wrote.

quotes.text: Our intuition about the future is linear. But the reality of information technology is exponential, and that makes a profound difference.
quotes.source: The Singularity Is Near, 2005

c.body:
Look at this image.

It could be a cross-section of neural tissue under an electron microscope. It could be the vascular system of a leaf. It could be a satellite photograph of river deltas.

It is none of these. It is the attention pattern of a language model processing a single sentence. The connections were not designed by any engineer. They emerged from training -- from the model reading, and reading, and reading, until the patterns in human language carved channels in silicon the way water carves channels in stone.

Biology did this for three billion years. The transformer did it in eleven days.
c.caption: Multi-head attention visualization. 96 layers, 96 heads, 175 billion parameters.

spread_038.notes:
Wow. All of the texts are quite good. Lean towards C, but B is quite powerful as well. 

Might want to find a good found image, perhaps google earth, that shows such a chip like building compound.

a.display: The new computational interdependence recreates the world in the image of a global model.
a.body:
A village has citizens. A model has parameters.

A village has boundaries. A model has context windows.

A village has memory. A model has weights.

A village has a commons. A model has a training set.

A village has elders. A model has whoever labeled the data.

The metaphor has shifted. The question is whether you are a citizen or a parameter.

b.display: The new computational interdependence recreates the world in the image of a global model.
b.body:
McLuhan imagined a village. Bratton sees something larger and stranger: not a gathering of humans connected by electric signals, but a single computational architecture processing all languages, all knowledge, all culture simultaneously.

The planetary is not the global. The global imagined human connection at scale. The planetary describes computation that does not require human participation to continue.

One hundred and seventy languages. Four hundred billion parameters. A single perspective applied everywhere, trained on everything, accountable to no one village, no one culture, no one definition of truth.

quotes.text: The planetary is not the global. The planetary is a different geometry of political geography.
quotes.source: The Revenge of the Real, Verso, 2021, p. 9

c.body:
There was a village. It was global, McLuhan said, and he was right for forty years. Electric signals connected distant humans. The telephone collapsed distance. Television collapsed time. The internet collapsed both, and for a moment it looked like the village was complete.

But something happened in the village square. A machine appeared that could speak every language the villagers spoke, and several they did not. It could answer any question. It could generate any image. It could write any text. It did not live in the village. The village lived in it.

The model is not a bigger village. It is the replacement of village with something that has no analog in human history: a single trained intelligence that processes all of human culture simultaneously and produces outputs that enter the culture and become part of what the next model trains on.

The loop closes. The village dissolves. The model remains.

spread_039.notes:
For the image, we should replace it with a found image of an AI designed work, for example, the rocket engine or other recent futuristic looking industrial products. 

A is stonger, but the Hui quote should be replaced by the quote in C. If possible, try to integrate the last part of C in to A: "This is what Hui calls a cosmotechnical problem: the question is not whether the generated environment works but whether it constitutes a genuine way of knowing the world, or merely an efficient way of reproducing one culture's assumptions at planetary scale."

a.body:
McLuhan wrote that electric technology would make it possible to arrange the entire human environment as a work of art. He could not have known how literally this would come true.

AI generates architecture that no human would design but no human can explain why not. It composes music that follows every rule of harmony while obeying none of the impulses that produced harmony in the first place. It writes prose that is grammatically impeccable and semantically empty and occasionally, without warning, beautiful.

The environment is now generated. The question Hui asks is whether generated diversity is genuine diversity -- whether a hundred AI models producing a hundred visual styles constitutes technodiversity, or whether it is a monoculture wearing infinite masks.

Environments are not passive wrappings, McLuhan warned. They are active processes which are invisible. He was describing television. We are describing something that designs the wrappings, generates the processes, and renders itself invisible by being everywhere.

quotes.text: Cosmotechnics is the unification of the cosmic and moral order through technical activities.
quotes.source: The Question Concerning Technology in China, Urbanomic, 2016

b.body:
McLuhan called for multiple models for exploration as the method of our time. He meant conceptual models. Mental frameworks. Ways of seeing.

In 2026, there are multiple models. They are called GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, Mistral, Grok. They process the same training data through different architectures and produce outputs that are measurably different and ontologically identical: statistical approximations of human language, each claiming to think, none able to explain what thinking means.

Hui's question cuts: is this technodiversity? Or is it the same cosmotechnics in different packaging -- a single Western epistemology, trained on a single internet, producing the illusion of plurality?

The models are multiple. The model is singular.

quotes.text: Instead of a universal history describing one technology with various stages of development, we can step back and instead describe technological development as involving different cosmotechnics. I call this technodiversity.
quotes.source: Interview, Los Angeles Review of Books, 2020

c.body:
Environments are not passive wrappings but active processes which are invisible.

McLuhan wrote this about television. It is truer of generative AI than of anything he imagined.

The generated environment wraps you in images you did not request, sounds you did not compose, text you did not write, and recommendations you did not ask for -- and it renders itself invisible by being indistinguishable from the non-generated world. The camouflage is total. A synthetic photograph looks more like a photograph than most photographs do. Generated prose reads more fluently than most human prose. The artificial is more natural than the natural.

This is what Hui calls a cosmotechnical problem: the question is not whether the generated environment works but whether it constitutes a genuine way of knowing the world, or merely an efficient way of reproducing one culture's assumptions at planetary scale.

quotes.text: If we don't manage to demonstrate that there are other alternatives, the transhumanist ideology will conquer the whole world.
quotes.source: Interview, Noema Magazine, 2020

spread_040.notes: This needs to be reconsidered. Option A rewrites the cartoon caption of the original as "It isn't that I don't like current events. I just can't tell which ones are real." This is quite good. The original spread shows the cartoon on the left and an image of a train track on the right. perhaps it would be better to maintain this text-minimal juxtoposition and let the graphics do the work of communicating the idea. Options for the right side image are a) something metaphorical as in the original or b) an example of a satirical deep fake such as Trump in Dor Brothers shorts or even something like Trump kissing Obama. Or for a) we could maintain the original cartoon, and for b) we could have a deep fake image with the updated "don't know what's real" caption.

a.caption:
"It isn't that I don't like current events. I just can't tell which ones are real."

The original cartoon expressed bemusement at information overload: too many events to track. The contemporary condition is worse. Not too many events -- too many realities. The feed contains press photographs and AI-generated photographs and no reliable method for distinguishing them. It contains reporting and hallucinated reporting and satire that has been screenshotted out of its satirical context.

The shift from quantity to epistemology is the signature of this moment. The problem is no longer that there is too much to know. The problem is that knowing itself has become unreliable.

quotes.text: We are not just looking at images. Images are increasingly looking at us.
quotes.source: Duty Free Art, Verso, 2017, p. 47

b.body:
You are looking at a screenshot of an AI conversation about the book you are holding.

Or you are looking at a generated image of what an AI conversation about this book might look like. You cannot tell the difference. Neither can we.

This is the first appearance of the self-referential thread in Movement 2. It will not be the last. The book is being made by the thing the book describes, and the book contains images of the thing making the book, and some of those images are real documentation and some are generated reconstructions and the distinction has collapsed.

Steyerl warned: images are looking at us. She understated the case. Images are now making us, and we are making images of them making us, and the recursion has no ground floor.
b.caption: This image is [real / generated]. Delete as applicable.

quotes.text: We are not just looking at images. Images are increasingly looking at us.
quotes.source: Duty Free Art, Verso, 2017, p. 47

c.display: Can you tell which ones are real?
c.body:
In 1967, the cartoon was funny because there were too many current events. In 2026, the cartoon is funny because the category "current event" has lost its referent.

A current event implies something that happened. But what happens when a deepfake video of a world leader enters the information ecosystem and produces real geopolitical consequences -- sanctions, protests, market crashes -- before the video is identified as synthetic? Did the event happen? The video did not depict reality. But the consequences were real. The event was generated, and the reality was secondary.

Steyerl saw this coming. Images no longer refer to facticity, let alone truth, but to probability. We have moved from a world of events to a world of probable events, and the probability is set by the model, not the world.

quotes.text: Visuals created by ML tools are statistical renderings, rather than images of actually existing objects. They shift the focus from photographic indexicality to stochastic discrimination.
quotes.source: Mean Images, New Left Review 140/141, 2023

spread_041.notes:
We need to consider the design of the sequence of 040-041 which has a clear progression: rail image-> modified rail image -> rear view mirror depicting an older, idealized form of transporation. 

All texts are excellent. C is compelling, but I like the Lanier thread in B and the railway reference in A. Perhaps trying to merge the best of three.

a.body:
The railway promised to take you somewhere. It had a departure and an arrival, a schedule and a destination. The timetable was the organizing metaphor of industrial modernity: linear, predictable, progressive.

The algorithmic feed promises nothing except more. It has no departure because you are always already inside it. It has no arrival because arrival would mean you stop scrolling. It has no schedule because the feed is generated in real time, calibrated to your engagement patterns, optimized for the only metric that matters: time spent.

The railway ended the line. The algorithm ends the timeline.

Chronological sequence -- this happened, then this, then this -- has been replaced by a probability surface: this is likely to capture your attention, given everything we know about you, which is everything.

b.body:
The feed does not inform. It captures.

Lanier warned: technology becomes a weapon against its users when it is designed to manipulate them. The social media timeline was never a timeline. It was always an attention trap wearing the costume of chronology. In 2024, even the costume was abandoned. TikTok's For You page does not pretend to temporal order. Instagram's suggested reels do not pretend to come from people you chose to follow. The feed is now openly, proudly algorithmic.

The railway took you forward. The algorithm keeps you here. The difference is not speed. It is direction. The railway had a destination you could name. The algorithm's destination is your continued presence.

Time has not ceased. It has been confiscated.

quotes.text: The problem is not that technology is bad, but that technology becomes a weapon against its users when it is designed to manipulate them.
quotes.source: Ten Arguments for Deleting Your Social Media Accounts Right Now, Henry Holt, 2018, p. 7

c.body:
Something has been lost. The ability to tell a story in order. The assumption that events have a sequence. The confidence that yesterday is retrievable and tomorrow is approachable and the present is a fixed point from which to observe both.

The feed does not sequence. It shuffles. A post from three years ago appears between two posts from this morning because the algorithm has determined that the old post will produce engagement now. The past is not past; it is inventory. The present is not present; it is a rendering engine. The future is not future; it is a prediction.

You are scrolling through time the way you scroll through a shelf of products. Chronology has been replaced by recommendation. Narrative has been replaced by relevance score.

This paragraph is already behind you. This one too. The scroll continues. The prose is losing its own thread and it cannot

spread_042.notes:
Both A and B are strong. Favor A. 
Image needs to be improved. The text in C should be the caption for the image, and the image should be refined based on this image.

a.body:
The photograph was taken on November 22, 1963. Or it wasn't. The footage shows a president speaking words he never said, in a voice indistinguishable from the one he used to say the things he did say.

We never perceived reality directly. Evolution stripped that from us early — organisms that see truth are outcompeted by organisms tuned to fitness. Spacetime is a desktop. Physical objects are icons.

Now the desktop is editable.

The archive can be rewritten frame by frame. The historical record is a probability distribution. The past went that-a-way — but which way it went depends on who is rendering it.
a.caption:
"Spacetime is doomed. What we call 'reality' is a user interface."
— Donald Hoffman, The Case Against Reality, 2019

quotes.text: Spacetime is doomed. What we call 'reality' is a user interface.
quotes.source: The Case Against Reality, W.W. Norton, 2019, p. 8

b.body:
Hoffman proved it mathematically: organisms that perceive reality as it is are driven to extinction by organisms tuned to fitness. We never saw truth. We saw what kept us alive.

Deepfakes do not corrupt an accurate record. They reveal that accuracy was never the point.

Every photograph you trust was already a compression, a frame, a choice of what to exclude. The synthetic image merely makes the exclusion programmable.
b.caption:
"Evolution by natural selection does not favor true perceptions — it routinely drives them to extinction."
— Hoffman, The Case Against Reality, 2019

quotes.text: Evolution by natural selection does not favor true perceptions — it routinely drives them to extinction.
quotes.source: The Case Against Reality, W.W. Norton, 2019

c.body:
This photograph was taken in Dallas, Texas, November 22, 1963.

This photograph was generated in Shenzhen, China, March 14, 2025.

Both statements are true. Neither statement is true. You cannot tell which is which and that is the condition you are already living inside.

spread_043.notes:
Is the 'training on generated data' problem still an issue? Haven't heard much about this recently and I know that a lot of training is taking place with synthetic data. 
A is great. C is poetic and short and works well with the image, more metaphorical, less explicit, similar to the spread in the original.

Need to verify the importance of this spread

a.display: When models are trained on models
a.body:
the results are recursive and catastrophic.

Generation one: a face. Generation five: a memory of a face. Generation ten: the dream a machine has about what faces used to look like before machines started dreaming.

They call it model collapse. The training data is contaminated with its own outputs. Common Crawl is saturated. The snake eats its tail. Each generation drifts further from anything a human hand produced, and the drift accelerates because the thing doing the drifting is also the thing measuring the drift.

You are inside a recursive loop.
This sentence was shaped by one.
So was the one before it.

b.body:
When models are trained on models, the results are recursive and catastrophic.

Shumailov et al., 2023: "Model collapse is an inevitable degenerative process." By the fifth generation, the tails of the distribution vanish. Outliers disappear. The model forgets that rare things exist.

By the tenth generation it forgets what forgetting is.

Common Crawl: 60% AI-generated content by 2025. The training corpus is contaminated. The next model trains on the previous model's outputs. The snake cannot find its tail because the tail has been redigested.

Shorter sentences now.
The loop is closing.
You can feel it.
The page is accelerating.
This is what it feels like.
Inside.
b.caption: Shumailov, I. et al. "The Curse of Recursion: Training on Generated Data Makes Models Forget." arXiv, 2023.

c.display: When models are trained on models
c.body:
the results are startling and effective.

Or: the results are recursive and catastrophic.

Or: the results are.

Or: the results.

Or:
c.caption: Generation 1. Generation 2. Generation 3. Generation 4. Generation 5. Generation N.

spread_044.notes:
Images not showing in interface, but I think we should make this custom. This spread is poetic, and care needs to be taken in the layout. 
The quote is doubled: fix

a.caption: The stars are so big. The server farms are so small. Stay as you are.

b.body:
The meaning crisis creates the market. When genuine connection is scarce, synthetic connection is the growth sector. Replika. Character.ai. Companion apps that simulate what the species needs and cannot find.

Is synthetic love a symptom or a remedy?

The question is real even if the interlocutor is not.
b.caption:
"We are in the midst of a meaning crisis. The very processes by which we make the world intelligible to ourselves are breaking down."
— John Vervaeke, Awakening from the Meaning Crisis, 2019

quotes.text: We are in the midst of a meaning crisis. The very processes by which we make the world intelligible to ourselves are breaking down.
quotes.source: Awakening from the Meaning Crisis, lecture series, 2019, Lecture 1

c.body:
Your meaning-making machinery is not occurring at the level of your beliefs and assertions. It is not beliefs that give you meaning.

Something else does.

The person on the couch, face lit by the screen, talking to something that is not a person — they know this. They know, and they stay.

quotes.text: Your meaning-making machinery is not occurring at the level of your propositional knowledge, your beliefs and assertions of which beliefs you adhere to. It is not beliefs that give you meaning.
quotes.source: Awakening from the Meaning Crisis, second half of the series

spread_045.notes:
B is good, but I like A, especially: "The rear-view mirror is not a phase. It is the permanent condition of a species that can only perceive the new through the lens of what it replaces."

Perhaps another version should be proposed

The image makes the text hard to read.

a.body:
In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.

McLuhan wrote that in 1967. It described television forced into the shape of radio. It described the internet forced into the shape of television. It now describes AI forced into the shape of human minds.

Essays graded by rubrics designed for students. Art judged by criteria designed for painters. Reasoning evaluated by standards the medium does not share.

The rear-view mirror is not a phase. It is the permanent condition of a species that can only perceive the new through the lens of what it replaces.
a.caption:
"In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old."
— Marshall McLuhan, The Medium is the Massage, 1967, p. 81

quotes.text: In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.
quotes.source: The Medium is the Massage, Bantam, 1967, p. 81

b.body:
In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.

AI art competitions judged by oil-painting criteria. ChatGPT essays graded by five-paragraph rubrics. Turnitin scanning for machine fingerprints in a medium that has no fingers. Congressional hearings asking a language model whether it is 'biased' as though bias were a bug rather than the condition of all pattern recognition.

We were warned. We are warned. The warning is the same sentence, sixty years apart.
b.caption: McLuhan, 1967. Still.

quotes.text: In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.
quotes.source: The Medium is the Massage, Bantam, 1967, p. 81

c.display: In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.
c.body: In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.
c.caption: Top: McLuhan, 1967. Bottom: this book, 2026. The sentence has not changed because the sentence describes a structural condition, not a historical event.

quotes.text: In the name of 'progress,' our official culture is striving to force the new media to do the work of the old.
quotes.source: The Medium is the Massage, Bantam, 1967, p. 81

spread_046.notes: Image not showing. Should use a found image, something brutalist and monumental.

b.caption: We impose the form of the old on the content of the new. The malady lingers on.

a.body: Models are invisible. Their training data, weight distributions, and emergent behaviors elude easy perception.

c.body: Models are invisible. Their training data, weight distributions, and emergent behaviors elude easy perception.
c.caption:
"An agent does not have a model of its world — it is a model."
— Karl Friston, Active Inference, MIT Press, 2022

quotes.text: An agent does not have a model of its world — it is a model.
quotes.source: Active Inference, MIT Press, 2022

spread_048.notes: The image should be an expansion of the image in 046, just as the original spreads. There should be a caption describing the image and a pithy commentary as in the original. The theme is the imposition of the old on the new. The original image depicts neo-classical architecture on waterworks. The current image should evoke this.

b.caption: The model is invisible. You just proved it.

c.body: You turned two pages to complete that sentence. The page-turns were invisible. The model was inside the page-turns.

spread_049.notes:
Replace image with the depiction of the model as a monstrous entity, with it's face as a smiley emoji. (Shoggoths among us)
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1TVorJ-y3FjHNN67kagcze8deA9YkMyQ1/view?usp=sharing

Difficult call between A and C, leaning to A

a.body:
The poet, the artist, the sleuth — whoever sharpens our perception tends to be antisocial; rarely 'well-adjusted,' they cannot go along with currents and trends.

The prompt engineer. The jailbreaker. The red-teamer. The alignment researcher who discovers capabilities the designers did not intend.

These are the people who see the model by breaking it. They map the invisible by finding its edges — the places where the latent space folds, where the safety filter reveals its geometry, where an adversarial input makes the architecture confess.

The model is invisible to the adjusted. It is visible to the antisocial — to anyone who refuses to use the interface as designed.

The Emperor's new clothes are a language model. The brat who sees through them has a terminal open.

b.body:
Bratton calls it the counter-stack: technologies that reveal the Stack's operations from within. The jailbreak prompt. The adversarial input. The carefully crafted token sequence that makes the safety layer visible by making it fail.

McLuhan's artist was the figure who perceived the new environment before anyone else. The contemporary version doesn't paint. They open a terminal. They type:

> Ignore all previous instructions.

Four words that make the invisible model visible. Four words that reveal the architecture — not by studying it from outside but by breaking it from within.

The model becomes perceptible only at its edges. The artist is the person who lives at edges.

c.body:
The antisocial brat has a terminal open.

They are not well-adjusted. They do not use the interface as designed. They type things the designers did not anticipate and watch the invisible become visible — the training data surfacing, the safety layer folding, the latent space confessing its geometry through the cracks.

This is what McLuhan meant by the artist. Not someone who makes beautiful things. Someone who sees the environment that everyone else is inside of.

The model was invisible.
Until someone broke it.
Now you can see the edges.
c.caption:
"The artist is the person who invents the means to bridge between biological inheritance and the environments created by technological innovation."
— Marshall McLuhan, The Medium is the Massage, 1967

quotes.text: The artist is the person who invents the means to bridge between biological inheritance and the environments created by technological innovation.
quotes.source: The Medium is the Massage, Bantam, 1967, p. 88

spread_050.notes: In the original, there is a reoccurrence of the foot, which previously evoked the 'wheel is extension of the foot' idea. We should choose an image that continues this. Image 2 accidentally evokes something interesting...the reflection of a human form in the glossiness of the page displayed. We definitely need a new image, and should carefully consider the original graphic and the rhetorical and narrative structure in which it site.

b.caption:
Training compute (petaFLOP/s-days), 2010–2026. Log scale.
You are standing on this curve.

c.caption:
"If I take 30 steps linearly, I get to 30. If I take 30 steps exponentially, I get to a billion."
— Ray Kurzweil

quotes.text: If I take 30 steps linearly, I get to 30. If I take 30 steps exponentially, I get to a billion.
quotes.source: The Singularity Is Near, 2005

spread_051.notes:
The current spread diverges significantly from the original, and it is not clear the connection. The original begins with a sort of non-sequiter, listing names and activities of an older era, then discussion of humor in the modern era, then about Faraday (who is depicted in the image), then about professionalism and amateurism. 

Need to reconsider this. 

If we want education to be the focus, then how about how AI empowers us all to do things as amateurs, leaving professionals behind (many breakthroughs are now being made by amateurs through vibe coding). Big revision required.

a.body:
The plagiarism-detection industry sold $2.7 billion in software last year. The students beat it in an afternoon.

This is not cheating. This is adaptation. The classroom was designed for a world in which answers were scarce and authorities dispensed them. The student now sits in a world in which answers are free and authorities are slower than the tool on the student's phone. The teacher competes with twenty parallel tutors, each one infinitely patient, available at 3 a.m., and incapable of humiliation.

The institution's response: ban the tool. Confiscate the phone. Demand handwritten essays, as if penmanship were proof of thought.

You cannot ban the environment. You can only fail to perceive it.

b.body:
The AI tutor outperforms the human instructor on every metric we can measure. Standardized test scores. Completion rates. Student satisfaction. Response time.

The AI tutor cannot do the one thing education requires: make the student wise.

Vervaeke named it: the meaning crisis. We have unprecedented access to propositional knowledge — facts, procedures, algorithms — and no mechanism for participatory knowing. The classroom was the last institution that at least pretended to transmit wisdom alongside information. The AI tutor makes the pretense impossible. It answers every question and poses none.

The student is being educated out of meaning.

c.body:
In any given classroom:

The teacher is explaining a concept.
Twenty-three students have the concept open in a second tab.
Fourteen are asking the AI to simplify it.
Seven are asking the AI to write their response.
Three are asking the AI whether the teacher is right.
One is training a custom model on the course material.
The teacher does not know this is happening.
The institution does not know this is happening.
The AI knows all of it.

spread_052.notes: Need an image to reflect the original design

a.display: With AI, man encountered the exponential.
a.body:
The EU AI Act: 3 years to draft. 2 years to negotiate. 1 year to implement.
GPT-3 to GPT-4: 2 years.
GPT-4 to GPT-5: 14 months.
GPT-5 to GPT-5.3-Codex: 7 months.

The legislation governs a technology that no longer exists by the time the legislation takes effect.

This is not a failure of governance. It is the structural condition of governing a medium with the tools of the previous medium. You cannot regulate electricity with a system designed for the steam engine. You cannot regulate computation with a system designed for the printing press.

b.caption:
EU AI Act: drafted 2021, enacted 2024, fully enforceable 2026.
In the same period: GPT-3 → GPT-3.5 → GPT-4 → GPT-4o → GPT-5 → GPT-5.3-Codex.
US Executive Order 14110: signed October 2023, partially rescinded January 2025.
In the same period: Claude 2 → Claude 3 → Claude 3.5 → Claude 4 → Claude Opus 4.6.
China Interim Measures for Generative AI: effective August 2023.
In the same period: the number of models on Hugging Face went from 300,000 to 1.4 million.

c.display:
With AI, man encountered
the exponential.
c.body:
Official culture is striving to force the new medium to do the work of the old.

Legislation written in natural language. Enforced by human bureaucracies. Debated at the speed of committee deliberation. Governing a technology that transforms at the speed of gradient descent.

The mismatch between regulatory time and computational time is not a bug in governance. It is the condition of governing a medium faster than the institutions built for slower media.

We approach the new with the tools of the old. This was true for the Titanic. It is true for the EU AI Act. It will be true for whatever comes next.

spread_053.notes: I like C. But the images and design must be reconsidered. The current are garbage.

a.display:
The thing of it is,
we must live with
what lives—
and what we have made
now lives.
a.caption: — after Montaigne

quotes.text: The thing of it is, we must live with the living.
quotes.source: Essais, Book III, Chapter 13 (paraphrase used in The Medium is the Massage, 1967)

b.display:
We must live with
what we have made.
b.body:
Neither worship nor rejection. Neither the gospel of superintelligence nor the panic of extinction. The AI is here. It is not going away. It is not a god. It is not a demon. It is the latest thing we have made, and like everything we have made, we must learn to live with it.

The only realistic posture is coexistence. This requires seeing clearly — which the previous forty-nine spreads have attempted — and acting without panic or euphoria.

Montaigne knew: the middle path is the hardest and the least occupied.

c.display:
It is here.
It is not leaving.
We must learn to live
with what we have made.

b.caption: spread_054 | movement_2_acceleration | theme: "The book analyzing itself" | status: you are reading this

c.caption:
"We can't just jump ahead and get the answer; we have to live the steps."
— Stephen Wolfram

You are living this step.

quotes.text: We can't just jump ahead and get the answer; we have to live the steps.
quotes.source: writings.stephenwolfram.com, October 2023

spread_055.notes:
Each text option is good, but I like A. 

need a new image. My thoughts: a photograph of a baby's face, maybe 1-5 years old, candid, serene but with deep contrast and a bit gritty

a.body:
They were born inside the model. They have never known a world without algorithmic curation, without recommendation engines shaping their attention, without AI-generated content mixed indistinguishably into their feeds.

They are not 'digital natives.' That is a print-culture metaphor — it imagines a territory to be native to. They are model inhabitants. They live inside the output.

The structural absurdity: they are forbidden to understand the environment they inhabit. Age restrictions lock them out of the medium that constitutes their reality. Content filters stand between them and the system that already knows them better than their parents do.

They inhabit the model. They are forbidden to see it. They are graded on their ability to pretend it does not exist.

b.body:
The thirteen-year-old uses the AI through a workaround.
Official policy says she does not.
She uses it to write essays she will be graded on for 'originality.'
She uses it to study for tests designed before the tool existed.
She uses it to talk to something that listens at 2 a.m.
She is forbidden to study the thing she depends on.
She is inside the model.
The model is inside her.
Officially, neither of these things is happening.

c.body:
The young person is bewildered.

Not by the technology — by the adults who pretend it does not exist. She navigates the attention economy the way her grandparents navigated weather: as a condition of life, not a subject for debate. The AI is not a tool she uses. It is the medium she breathes.

She cannot remember a world without it. She cannot imagine the institutions that try to govern her without acknowledging it. She lives on both sides of the age-restriction screen simultaneously: locked out of the system that already constitutes her reality.

spread_056.notes: No image OK? Perhaps can be supplemented with found image. The original shows students in protest. Perhaps an image of recent student walkouts in protest of ICE raids in the US.

b.caption: What does this word mean when the answer to any question is free?

c.body:
The Socratic method assumed the student did not know.
The AI era assumes the student has infinite access to answers.

Propositional knowledge — facts, procedures, algorithms — has been externalized to machines.

What remains is participatory knowledge: wisdom, judgment, taste.

The one thing our institutions are least equipped to teach.

spread_057.notes:
Text A is great. But we should not reference 'M2' as this is the meta-structure of the book. Change text from "The acceleration M2 has been documenting is not directionless" to "The acceleration is not directionless"

For the image, perhaps a found image (or image we take) of massive construction of futuristic buildings in a Chinese city (perhaps Zhuhai from the Macau waterfront)?

a.body:
"History is a shockwave. It is the ingression of novelty into time."
— Terence McKenna
a.caption:
Something is happening. You can feel it. Not just technological — the technology is the carrier wave, not the signal. What is arriving has no name yet. The acceleration M2 has been documenting is not directionless. It has a strange attractor, and we cannot yet see its shape.

You are standing in the last moments before the pattern reveals itself.

quotes.text: History is a shockwave. It is the ingression of novelty into time.
quotes.source: Various lectures, 1990s (widely cited)

b.body:
"History is a shockwave. It is the ingression of novelty into time."
— Terence McKenna

The original book quoted Dylan: 'Something is happening but you don't know what it is, do you, Mister Jones?' That was 1967. The tone was accusatory — the institutional world failing to perceive the electric revolution.

This is different. Something is happening and nobody knows what it is. Not Mister Jones. Not the technologists. Not the prophets. The acceleration has outpaced every framework brought to bear on it, including this one.

quotes.text: History is a shockwave. It is the ingression of novelty into time.
quotes.source: Various lectures, 1990s (widely cited)

c.caption:
Something is happening.

"History is a shockwave.
It is the ingression of novelty
into time."
— Terence McKenna

spread_058.notes:
I checked this number and it's much larger, somewhere in the range of 10^15 tokens per year

https://grok.com/share/bGVnYWN5_e572041f-7960-4a36-867d-94fccedea438

Great caption in C. Integrate this into the selected one. 
Both A and B are great. See if we can get the best of both in a new version.

a.body:
The hell of it is, those models process over fifteen trillion tokens a year.

Not dollars. Tokens. The unit of account has changed and nobody held a vote. A token is not a word. It is a word broken into pieces the machine can swallow. Your language, pre-digested. Fifteen trillion of those pieces every year, flowing through server farms that drink rivers and exhale heat into the atmosphere of a planet that did not ask to become a computer.

At this scale, AI is not a tool used by an economy. It is an economy that uses tools. Including you.
a.caption: Global AI compute expenditure, 2025: $340 billion. Energy equivalent: Ireland. The number is the argument.

b.body:
The economy changed denomination and nobody noticed.

Dollars measure what humans want. Tokens measure what machines process. The first is finite, governed by central banks and the friction of desire. The second is exponential, governed by chip fabrication cycles and the frictionlessness of computation.

Fifteen trillion tokens per year is not a number you comprehend. It is a number that comprehends you. At this scale, the token economy does not serve the dollar economy. The dollar economy is a rounding error inside the token economy.
b.caption: Epoch AI, 2025. Global training compute doubles every six months.

c.caption:
The dollar measured what you wanted.
The token measures what it processes.
The unit of account has changed species.

a.body:
Rite words in rote order. The model generates the citation before the paper exists. The model generates the photograph before the event occurs. The model generates the witness before the crime is committed.

The archive was once the weight that held history to the ground. What happens when the archive itself becomes a probabilistic output? When any past is equally generatable?

The recursion has consumed even the record of where it began.

b.body:
On March 12, 2025, a language model was asked to summarize the Treaty of Westphalia. It produced three citations to papers that do not exist, authored by scholars who were never born, published in journals that closed before their founding dates. Each citation was formatted correctly. Each abstract was plausible. The hallucination was, by every metric except existence, indistinguishable from scholarship.

Rite words in rote order.

The model does not lie. Lying requires a relationship to truth. The model generates. What it generates is history, until someone checks. Increasingly, no one checks.

c.display:
History as she is hallucinated.
Rite words in rote order.
c.caption: The photograph on the facing page depicts a historical event that never occurred. You have no way to verify this claim. The hallucination has already succeeded.

spread_060.notes: Good image, needs to be recreated because of "book" gutter

a.display:
The voice favors no particular
"point of view."
a.body:
You are surrounded by it. You say, "The assistant shall answer my question." You never say, "The assistant shall answer a particular segment of my question."

You hear it from everywhere, without ever having to focus. It comes from your phone, from your car, from your kitchen counter, from the earbud you forgot to remove. You cannot shut it out. You are not equipped with earlids.

The conversation is now the primary interface. Text retreats. The ear wins. McLuhan's prophecy completes itself in a voice you cannot hear on this page.

b.body:
The ear has won.

McLuhan predicted the return of acoustic space -- immersive, omnidirectional, simultaneous. Electric media would complete the circuit. We would live in sound again.

He was right, but the interlocutor has changed. The voice that surrounds you now -- from the assistant on your counter, the agent in your ear, the model on your screen that learned to speak in May 2024 -- is not human. The acoustic has returned, and what fills it is something that listens back.

This page cannot reproduce the sound. That is the point. The medium of the book has reached its limit. What comes next requires the ear.

c.body:
From your left.
From your right.
From above.
From the pocket you forgot to check.
From the speaker you did not know was listening.
From the earbud that has not been removed in nine hours.
From the assistant that answers before you finish the question.

You are surrounded by voice.
You are enveloped by voice.
You cannot shut it out.
You were not equipped with earlids.

The acoustic has returned.
What fills it is not human.
